notes: Official Treasury product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
As a self-employed UX consultant, you have a unique opportunity to use health insurance as a tax-lever. Since your income arrives unevenly, checking your **HSA eligibility** and **Premium Tax Credit (PTC)** thresholds is the best way to protect your cash flow.

Based on your spending data, you are currently paying `415` per month to **HealthCare.gov**. If this is for a 2026 silver or bronze plan, here is your checklist for insurance and HSA optimization.

### 1. Verify HSA Eligibility (The "Triple Advantage")
An HSA is arguably the best account for a freelancer because it provides a **triple tax advantage**: deductible contributions, tax-free growth, and tax-free withdrawals for medical costs. To use it, you must be enrolled in a qualifying **High Deductible Health Plan (HDHP)**.

For 2026, check if your marketplace plan meets these IRS requirements:
*   **Minimum Annual Deductible:** At least `1,700` for self-only coverage.
*   **Maximum Out-of-Pocket:** No more than `8,500` for self-only coverage.
*   **Contribution Limit:** You can contribute up to `4,400` for 2026.

> **Warning:** You cannot contribute to an HSA if you are also covered by a non-HDHP plan (like a spouse's PPO) or a general-purpose Flexible Spending Account (FSA).

### 2. Monitor the "400% FPL Cliff"
Since you are in Texas (a non-Medicaid-expansion state), your **Premium Tax Credit (PTC)** is highly sensitive to your income. 
*   **The Threshold:** In 2026, the "subsidy cliff" is back. If your Modified Adjusted Gross Income (MAGI) exceeds **400% of the Federal Poverty Level** (`62,600` for a single filer based on the 2025 FPL of `15,650`), your credit drops to zero immediately.
*   **The Strategy:** If your consultant income spikes toward the end of the year, you can lower your MAGI and "save" your health insurance subsidy by contributing to a **Solo 401(k)** or **Traditional IRA**. This can sometimes save you thousands in avoided premium repayments.

### 3. Claim the Self-Employed Health Insurance Deduction
Because you buy your own insurance and have no employer offer, you can likely deduct **100% of your premiums** (`4,980/year` based on your current payments) as an "above-the-line" deduction on Form 1040.
*   **Why it matters:** This deduction reduces your income tax, but unlike business equipment or software, it does **not** reduce your 15.3% Self-Employment (SE) tax.
*   **Eligibility:** You can only claim this for months where neither you nor your spouse (if applicable) were eligible for an employer-subsidized health plan.

---

### Action Checklist
- [ ] **Confirm HDHP Status:** Log in to HealthCare.gov or check your Plan Summary to see if it specifically says "HSA-compatible."
- [ ] **Open a Personal HSA:** If your plan is compatible, open an HSA at a provider like Fidelity or Lively to start making tax-deductible contributions.
- [ ] **Track MAGI vs. FPL:** Since you want "tax-safe cashflow," keep a running total of your net profit. If you approach `62,600`, consider moving cash into a retirement account to stay under the subsidy cliff.
- [ ] **Check for Enhanced Subsidies:** Verify if you qualify for cost-sharing reductions (CSRs), which are available if your MAGI is below 250% of FPL and you use a **Silver** plan.

**One final note on your cash flow:** since you value low-admin systems, you don't need to max out the HSA all at once. You can make contributions at any time up until the tax filing deadline (usually April 15 of the following year), allowing you to wait until you have a high-income month to fund the account.

[Displayed Treasury Insight card]
In Texas, exceeding the 400% FPL cliff ($62,600) could cost you your entire premium subsidy; a late-year Solo 401(k) contribution is your best tool to stay below it.
Highlights: 62,600; Solo 401(k) contribution.
